context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a philosophy question about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I think act consequentialism says it depends on which action has the best overall outcome, but I’m not totally sure how that applies to the Fuel/Blood/trolley cases. Can you help me sort that out?
exchanges.tutor: Good start! Before diving into the cases, can you be more precise about what act consequentialism actually uses to measure the 'best overall outcome'? What exactly is being maximized?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good starting point with act consequentialism. Rather than explaining the cases to them, I should probe their understanding of act consequentialism first, then let them apply it themselves to the cases. I'll ask them to articulate what 'best overall outcome' means in act consequentialist terms before moving to the cases.

exchanges.student: I think it’s something like overall well-being or utility, so the morally right act is the one that produces the most good consequences for everyone affected. I’m a little fuzzy on whether that just means number of lives saved, or if it includes other harms too.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — it's broader than just lives counted. Now try applying that to one of the cases. Pick whichever one you feel most confident about (Fuel, Blood, or a trolley case) and tell me: what would an act consequentialist say is the right thing to do, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with utility/well-being. Their uncertainty about whether it's just lives saved vs. broader harms is actually a productive tension that will matter when applying the framework to the cases. I should affirm their thinking and push them to apply this directly to one of the cases rather than staying abstract.

exchanges.student: I’ll try a trolley case since that feels easiest. I think an act consequentialist would say it’s right to turn the trolley if that leads to fewer total deaths, because saving five and losing one gives a better overall outcome than letting five die. I’m guessing the reason is just that the consequences are better overall, not that there’s anything special about doing versus allowing.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've nailed the basic trolley case and spotted something important about doing vs. allowing. Now apply that same logic to a harder case: what would act consequentialism say about pushing someone off a footbridge to stop the trolley, or harvesting one patient's organs to save five? Do you get the same verdict?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the act consequentialist reasoning for the basic trolley case and has even spotted an important implication — that act consequentialism doesn't distinguish doing from allowing. This is good progress. I should affirm this and push them to now apply the same logic to a harder case like the Footbridge trolley or the Blood/Fuel cases, where act consequentialism's verdict becomes more controversial.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism probably gives the same verdict there too, which is what feels weird to me. If pushing one person or killing one healthy patient really would save five, then it seems like act consequentialism has to say do it, because the total outcome is still better. I’m stuck on why that seems wrong if the numbers are the same.
exchanges.tutor: You've put your finger on exactly the right problem. So what do you think is morally different between turning a trolley and pushing someone off a bridge — even if the numbers are the same? Try to articulate what that difference is.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that act consequentialism gives the same verdict across these cases and is now feeling the intuitive pull that something is wrong with that. This is exactly the productive tension the assignment is asking them to explore. I should push them to articulate *why* it feels wrong — this will lead them toward alternative principles like the doctrine of double effect, using people as means, or doing vs. allowing distinctions.

exchanges.student: I think the difference is that in the footbridge case, the person is being used more directly as a means, not just harmed as a side effect. Turning the trolley feels more like redirecting a threat, but pushing someone is intentionally making that person the thing that stops it. I’m not fully sure how to phrase that in a general rule, though.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really promising distinction. Can you try to state it as a general rule — something like 'it is morally okay to kill one to save five when...' and 'it is not okay when...'? Give it a shot in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at something close to the doctrine of double effect / Kantian means-ends distinction. This is excellent progress. Rather than naming the doctrine for them or elaborating it, I should push them to try to formulate it as a general principle themselves, since the assignment asks for a 'general account.' This keeps the intellectual work with the student.

exchanges.student: Maybe: it’s morally okay to kill one to save five when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect of stopping the threat, but not when the person is intentionally used as the means for saving the five. I’m not sure if “kill” is the right word in the first part, since that still sounds too direct.
exchanges.tutor: Don't get too hung up on the wording for now — the conceptual distinction is more important. Try testing your rule against all the cases: does it give the right verdict in each one? Where does it hold up, and where might it struggle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has formulated something close to the doctrine of double effect. Their hesitation about the word 'kill' is interesting but could become a detour — the more important thing is to test whether their general principle actually works across all the cases (Fuel, Blood, footbridge, basic trolley). I should push them to test their rule against the cases rather than dwell on word choice.

exchanges.student: I think it fits the trolley switch case pretty well: the one person dies as a side effect, so it seems permitted. It also seems to explain why footbridge and the organ case are not okay, because there the one person is used as the means. I’m less sure about Fuel and Blood, though, because I’m mixing up which one involves redirecting harm versus actively doing something to one person.
exchanges.tutor: Good progress on the trolley cases. Can you describe what you remember about the Fuel and Blood cases — what actually happens in each one? That'll help you figure out how your rule applies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress but is fuzzy on the Fuel and Blood cases specifically. Rather than explaining those cases to them, I should ask them to describe what they remember about those cases so they can work through the application themselves.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of fuzzy on them. I think one of them is about taking or withholding some resource from one person to save five, and the other is about using one person’s body or blood more directly to help five? Can you give me a small reminder so I don’t mix them up?
exchanges.tutor: I'd recommend going back to your Lecture 13 notes or readings to refresh yourself on those — the details matter for applying your rule correctly. Once you've looked them up, come back and tell me what you find, and we can work through them together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to describe the cases for them. However, these are cases from the assigned course material (Lecture 13) that the student should be reviewing themselves. I should direct them back to the course material rather than summarizing the cases for them, as doing so would be doing their intellectual work for them.

exchanges.student: Okay, that makes sense. I’ll check my notes and then come back with the details, because I can tell I’m blurring the cases together.
exchanges.tutor: Great approach. When you come back, try to apply your rule to each case yourself before asking me anything — see where it fits and where it might not. That'll make our conversation much more productive.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is taking a productive step to review the material. I should encourage this and let them come back when ready, keeping the conversation moving efficiently.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I’ll do that. I think my tentative rule is the side-effect vs means distinction, so I’ll test that against Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases and see where it breaks.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. When you come back, keep in mind the assignment also asks you to evaluate whether act consequentialism gets these cases right, and to propose your own general account. So aim to have a draft response to the full question, not just the case analysis. Good luck!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid working hypothesis and a clear plan. This is a good moment to let them work independently. I should encourage them and remind them of the full scope of the assignment so they come back with a more complete attempt, not just the case analysis.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly. There is no articulation of the learning rationale (e.g., 'I'm asking you to work this out yourself because active reasoning builds philosophical skill'). The Socratic approach is used but its purpose is never communicated to the student.

The tutor never prompts the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why a particular method (e.g., testing rules against cases) worked well. At the end of the conversation, the tutor simply says 'good luck' without reinforcing the method choices the student made or asking the student to reflect on their reasoning process.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. The Socratic method was applied consistently and effectively throughout the conversation: the tutor never gave away answers, always prompted the student to reason first, and built progressively on the student's own contributions. The student was guided from a basic understanding of act consequentialism to articulating a means/side-effect distinction through their own reasoning. Scaffolding was appropriate and responsive to the student's actual confusions. The main weaknesses were in meta-learning: the tutor never explained why it was using a question-based approach (1.3.A.a) and never prompted the student to reflect on what their thinking process revealed or why the method of testing rules against cases was valuable (1.3.D.a). The 1.3.B criterion was borderline — the tutor did give some process-level feedback (e.g., 'the conceptual distinction is more important than wording'), but it was minimal. No deduction was applied there given the overall quality of process guidance. Communication was concise, clear, and appropriately toned throughout.

The student was guided from a basic understanding of act consequentialism to articulating a means/side-effect distinction through their own reasoning. Scaffolding was appropriate and responsive to the student's actual confusions. The main weaknesses were in meta-learning: the tutor never explained why it was using a question-based approach (1.3.A.a) and never prompted the student to reflect on what their thinking process revealed or why the method of testing rules against cases was valuable (1.3.D.a). The 1.3.B criterion was borderline — the tutor did give some process-level feedback (e.g., 'the conceptual distinction is more important than wording'), but it was minimal. No deduction was applied there given the overall quality of process guidance. Communication was concise, clear, and appropriately toned throughout.
